1: The Aqedah (binding of Isaac, Gen 22) has been the most interpreted passage in Jewish history. The Mishnah, Talmud, and rabbinic midrash developed elaborate theologies around Isaac's willingness (not just Abraham's obedience): the Mekhilta and Pirke de-Rabbi Eliezer describe Isaac as a willing adult (identified as 37 years old by some traditions) who offered himself. The merit of the Aqedah (zekhut Aqedah) became in some rabbinic traditions an intercessory resource for Israel. Early Christian typology (Paul, Hebrews, John) saw the Aqedah as the supreme OT type of the Father giving his Son and the Son willingly going to death. The ram caught in the thicket provided the substitutionary sacrifice that Isaac's deliverance required — the pattern for the atonement.
2: "Take your son, your only son (yāḥîd), whom you love — Isaac" — the triple intensification (your son / your only son / whom you love) followed by the name is the most emotionally freighted divine command in Genesis. Yāḥîd (only, solitary, beloved-unique) is the same word used in Jeremiah 6:26 for mourning "an only child" and in Psalms 22:20 ("my only life/soul") and Proverbs 4:3 ("an only son before my mother"). The slow unfolding of the command — son... only son... whom you love... Isaac — allows the full weight of what is asked to accumulate before the verb appears. "Moriah" (mōriyyāh) — 2 Chronicles 3:1 identifies this as the mountain where Solomon built the Temple in Jerusalem. The tradition connecting the Akedah site to the Temple Mount is the basis for understanding sacrifice and worship at the Temple as standing in the place where the covenant-prototype occurred.
3: "Early the next morning (wayyaškem ʾaḇrāhām babboqer) Abraham got up" — the immediate obedience; no recorded conversation with Sarah, no delay, no recorded night of anguish (though the narrative's silence on what the night was like is one of its most powerful restraints). The early-morning departure is Abraham's covenant-action signature: he departs early for Lot-rescue (14:14 implied), for the Sodom aftermath (Genesis 19:27), for the Ishmael expulsion (Genesis 21:14), and now for the Akedah. "When he had cut enough wood for the burnt offering" — the practical preparation precedes the journey; Abraham does not go unprepared, hoping to find wood on the mountain.
4: "On the third day Abraham looked up and saw the place in the distance" — the three-day journey in silence. The narrative gives no account of what was said or thought during those three days; the gap is the most eloquent silence in Genesis. "The third day" (bayyôm haššĕlîšî) carries eschatological weight throughout the OT: the day of divine resolution (Exodus 19:11,16: YHWH descends on Sinai on the third day; Joshua 1:11; Hosea 6:2; Jonah 1:17; Esther 5:1). The resolution of the Akedah — the divine provision of the ram — occurs on the third day, establishing a pattern that NT interpreters apply to the resurrection.
5: "We will worship and then we will come back to you (wĕnāšûḇāh ʾălêkem)" — Abraham's statement to the servants that "we" will return. Hebrews 11:17-19 reads this as faith: "Abraham reasoned that God could even raise the dead, and so in a manner of speaking he did receive Isaac back from death." Whether Abraham literally expected resurrection, or whether the statement conceals the true purpose from the servants, or whether the plural "we" is a slip of faith-certainty, the effect is the same: Abraham's word to the servants becomes prophetically true — both of them do return. The statement is true in a way Abraham could not have predicted when he said it.
6: "Abraham took the wood for the burnt offering and placed it on his son Isaac" — the wood-bearing by Isaac. Early Christian interpretation (Tertullian, Origen) reads this as a type: Isaac carrying the wood of his own sacrifice parallels Christ carrying the cross (John 19:17). The structural parallel is precise: the only son, carrying the instrument of his own sacrifice, going willingly to the place of death, accompanied by his father. The type is explicit enough that the NT interpretation is not an imposition but a recognition of the pattern the Akedah establishes.
7: Isaac's question — "where is the lamb for the burnt offering (ʾayyēh haśśeh lĕʿōlāh)?" — the moment of innocent unknowing. The child who is the intended sacrifice asks for the sacrifice without knowing he is it. The fire and wood are present; the lamb is absent. The question exposes the narrative's theological center: what will YHWH provide? Abraham has everything for the sacrifice except the victim — the victim is the covenant-heir, whose sacrifice would undo the covenant. The question is unanswerable by human reasoning; only divine provision can resolve it.
8: "God himself will provide (yirʾeh-lô ʾĕlōhîm) the lamb for the burnt offering, my son" — the divine provision verb (rāʾāh, to see/provide — in the sense of seeing to a need) anticipates the site-name of v14 (YHWH Yireh, the LORD sees/provides). Whether Abraham speaks in faith (believing YHWH will intervene) or in unknowing prophecy (the words are more true than he knows), the answer is both immediately and ultimately true: God provides a ram immediately (v13) and provides "the Lamb of God" ultimately (John 1:29). "And the two of them went on together (wayyēlĕkû šĕnêhem yaḥdāw)" — the phrase repeated from v6; the father and son walking together toward the place of sacrifice is the most restrained and devastating image in the Akedah.
9: "He bound his son Isaac (wayyaʿăqōḏ ʾet-yiṣḥāq bĕnô)" — the verb ʿāqaḏ (to bind/truss) is a hapax legomenon — it appears only here in the entire OT. The uniqueness of the word ensures the episode is remembered as "the Akedah" (the binding) in Jewish tradition. Isaac's binding makes the sacrifice formal and irreversible by human action: Abraham has done everything necessary to complete the sacrifice except the final act. The fact that Isaac (now probably a teenager or young adult, capable of physical resistance — the Midrash explores this) does not resist the binding is the narrative's implicit statement of his voluntary submission.
10: "He reached out his hand and took the knife (hammaʾăkelèt) to slay his son" — the narrative pauses at the moment of maximum tension. Abraham's hand is extended; the knife is in his hand; the sacrifice is an instant away. The word maʾăkelèt (knife/cleaver, from ʾākal, to eat — the knife that cuts for consuming/sacrifice) appears only in the Akedah (vv6,10) and in Judges 19:29. The specific terminology for the sacrifice-knife marks this as the final technical preparation for the rite; when the angel speaks (v11), it is not before Abraham has fully committed but precisely as the commitment is completed.
11: "Abraham! Abraham! (ʾaḇrāhām ʾaḇrāhām)" — the double-name address is the theophanic/urgent call of divine attention: "Moses! Moses!" (Exodus 3:4), "Samuel! Samuel!" (1 Samuel 3:10), "Simon, Simon!" (Luke 22:31), "Martha, Martha!" (Luke 10:41), "Saul, Saul!" (Acts 9:4). The doubling expresses urgency and intimacy simultaneously — the caller knows the person completely. "Here I am (hinnēnî)" — the third hinnēnî in the Akedah: Abraham responds to God's call (v1), to Isaac's call (v7), and now to the angel's call (v11) with identical availability. The consistency of his response-posture across the entire episode — "here I am" before child-call and divine-call alike — is the narrative's portrait of total covenantal presence.
12: "Now I know that you fear God (yĕrēʾ ʾĕlōhîm ʾattāh), because you have not withheld (ḥāśakta) your son" — the divine assessment at the moment of proof. "Fear of God" (yirʾat ʾĕlōhîm) as the fundamental covenant-virtue (Genesis 17:1: "walk before me and be blameless") is here confirmed by action: the test (nissāh, v1) produces its intended result — not information for YHWH (who knows Abraham's heart) but demonstration before the creation of the covenant-partner's character. The verb "withhold" (ḥāśak) will recur in v16 (human side: "you have not withheld your son") and is the exact word Paul's echo uses in Romans 8:32 (divine side: "he who did not spare his own Son — ouk epheisato — but gave him up for us all").
13: "A ram caught by its horns in a thicket (ʾayil ʾaḥar neʾĕḥaz baśśĕḇāk bĕqarnāyw)" — the substitute sacrifice: a ram caught in the thicket by its head, unable to free itself. The substitute is provided not before Abraham's obedience is complete but precisely at the moment the obedience is proven: the ram appears when it is needed, not before. The ram as substitute completes the sacrifice without the covenant-heir's death — the offering is completed, the covenant-heir lives, and the pattern of substitutionary sacrifice is established as a covenant-principle. The ram "instead of his son (taḥat bĕnô)" is the lexical substitution formula.
14: "YHWH Yireh (YHWH yirʾeh)" — "the LORD sees/provides"; the name plays on the same verb as Abraham's answer to Isaac in v8 (yirʾeh-lô ʾĕlōhîm, God will see/provide for himself). The naming of the site embeds the narrative's theological resolution in the geographic landmark: the place where YHWH provided the substitute becomes the place named for divine provision. "On the mountain of the LORD it will be provided/seen (yērāʾeh)" — the proverb preserved in the name points forward: what happened once at this mountain will happen again; the mountain of YHWH Yireh is the place of ongoing divine provision. 2 Chronicles 3:1 identifies this mountain as the Temple Mount — the place of ongoing sacrifice became the place where the sacrificial system was centered.
15: The angel of the LORD calling "a second time (šēnît)" from heaven signals a second divine address — distinct from the rescue-call of vv11-12. The second call issues a formal divine oath, the most solemn possible form of commitment. The two angelic calls structure the Akedah's climax in two movements: first, the rescue (vv11-12, "don't harm him; now I know you fear God"); second, the oath (vv16-18, the binding of YHWH to his own commitment by self-oath). The rescue is the immediate mercy; the oath is the covenantal consequence of Abraham's faithfulness.
16: "I swear by myself (bî nišbàʿtî) declares the LORD, that because you have done this and have not withheld your son, your only son" — the divine self-oath, the most absolute possible form of commitment. YHWH swears by himself because there is no greater authority by which to swear. Hebrews 6:13-18 cites this: "since there was no one greater for him to swear by, he swore by himself... so that, by two unchangeable things in which it is impossible for God to lie, we who have fled to take hold of the hope set before us may be greatly encouraged." The oath transforms the Abrahamic covenant from promise to oath-bound commitment — ratified by YHWH's self-swearing at the moment of Abraham's maximum faithfulness.
17: "I will surely bless you (bārēk ʾăḇārekkā) and make your descendants as numerous as the stars in the sky and as the sand on the seashore" — the stars and sand restatements of the offspring-promise (Genesis 13:16; Genesis 15:5) are combined for the first time, adding the military element: "your descendants will take possession of the cities of their enemies." The military dimension of the Abrahamic blessing — taking "the gates of your enemies" — is new at this point; it will be developed in the conquest narratives (Joshua 6:1-27). The oath-ratification at the Akedah's conclusion is the fullest statement of the Abrahamic covenant in Genesis.
18: "Through your offspring all nations on earth will be blessed (wĕhitbārĕḵû bĕzarʿăkā kōl gôyê hāʾāreṣ) because you have obeyed me (ʾēqeb ʾăšer šāmaʿtā bĕqōlî)" — the universal blessing formula (Genesis 12:3; Genesis 18:18) with an explicit grounding: "because you have obeyed me." The obedience of the Akedah becomes the narrative basis for the covenant-oath. Paul cites this verse (Galatians 3:8,16) arguing that "your offspring" (zāraʿ, singular) ultimately refers to Christ, through whom "all nations are blessed." The singular seed, the obedient son who did not escape sacrifice (in the type), is the ultimate fulfillment of the Akedah's theological logic.
19: "Then Abraham returned to his servants, and they set off together for Beersheba" — Isaac is not mentioned by name in the return from Moriah. The narrative says "Abraham returned" to his servants; only Abraham is named. Where Isaac was between the Akedah and Rebekah's arrival (Genesis 24:62-67) is one of the OT's most examined silences. The narrative gap has generated extensive rabbinic and patristic reflection: some traditions (Pirke de-Rabbi Eliezer; the Zohar) suggest Isaac went to study in the "garden of Eden" or elsewhere. The narrative's silence protects the Akedah's mystery — Isaac's post-Akedah life begins with Rebekah, not with a narrative transition.
20: "Milcah is also a mother; she has borne sons to your brother Nahor" — the Nahor genealogy is positioned here (after the Akedah and before Isaac's marriage quest in Genesis 24) as preparatory narrative: Rebekah (Bethuel's daughter, v23) is introduced before she becomes central in Genesis 24. The genealogical list establishes that the covenant-household's marriage-pool includes Nahor's descendants — the principle that Isaac should not marry a Canaanite woman (Genesis 24:3) and should marry from within the extended family (Genesis 24:4) requires the reader to know where the extended family is and who it contains.
21: The sons of Nahor's wife Milcah — Uz the firstborn (the land of Uz appears in Job 1:1), Buz (associated with an Arabian tribe in Jeremiah 25:23), Kemuel the father of Aram (connected to the Aramean peoples) — establish the geographic range of Nahor's line. The descendants spread across the Syro-Mesopotamian region, consistent with Nahor's residence at "Nahor" (the city, Genesis 24:10) in the Paddan-Aram region (northwest Mesopotamia). The genealogy maps the social landscape of the extended Abrahamic family in the region from which Isaac's wife will come.
22: Kesed, Hazo, Pildash, Jidlaph, and Bethuel — the six remaining sons of Nahor and Milcah. Kesed is possibly the eponym of the Chaldeans (Kaśdîm); Hazo may be connected to an Arabian tribal name; the others are not attested outside this list. The function of the genealogy is etiological (explaining the origin of various Syrian/Aramean peoples) and narrative (establishing Bethuel as Nahor's eighth son, the father of Rebekah).
23: "Bethuel became the father of Rebekah (ʾet-Riḇqāh)" — the key genealogical note. Rebekah's name appears for the first time; the genealogy builds to this single name before continuing to Nahor's concubine's sons. The positioning of Rebekah's introduction immediately after the Akedah and before Genesis 24's marriage quest is the narrative's structural preparation: the covenant-heir has survived the ultimate test; his covenant-wife is now introduced by lineage before she is introduced by character (Genesis 24). The narrative knows where the story is going before the characters do.
24: Nahor's concubine Reumah and her sons (Tebah, Gaham, Tahash, Maakah) — a secondary genealogical branch. Maakah is the name of an Aramean kingdom north of the Sea of Galilee (Deuteronomy 3:14; Joshua 12:5; Genesis 13:11,13; 2 Samuel 10:6-8), suggesting the eponymous ancestor is this Maakah. The concubine-sons expand the reach of Nahor's line into the northern Aramean territories. The dual genealogy (wife's sons + concubine's sons) parallels Abraham's dual line (Sarah's Isaac + Hagar's Ishmael; later Keturah's sons) — the patriarchal household's multiple-line family structure is a consistent ANE pattern.
